coredata.dc:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots for sensitive detection, intracellular imaging of Al3+
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Depeng
Yan, Fanyong
Luo, Yunmei
Ye, Qianghua
Zhou, Siyushan
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple and effective method was designed to synthesize hydrophobic carbon dots. Subsequently, amphiphilic fluorescent carbon dots (A-CDs) were synthesized by further surface modification. The result A-CDs show excellent optical properties with a quantum yield of 16.9%. It was interestingly found that morin (MR) and its fluorescent metal-ion complex (MR-Al3+) can successfully coordinate on the surface of A-CDs, the emission of A-CDs completely overlapped the absorption peak of MR-Al3+. Thus, the prepared A-CDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for Al3+ based on a fluorescence resonance energy transfer process. The sensing platform can realize real-time detection of Al3+ within 0.5 min. The fluorescence signals of the system were linearly correlated with the concentration of Al3+ over a range of 8–20 μM, with a detection limit of 0.113 μM. The method was also successfully applied to image the distribution of Al3+ in Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Al3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of A-CDs (A). XPS survey scan of A-CDs (B). The enlarged regions for Br3d (C). FTIR spectrum of CTAB and A-CDs (D).
Absorption (a) and fluorescence emission (b) spectra of A-CDs (A). Fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength from 300 to 380 nm in 10 nm increments) of A-CDs (B).
Schematic illustration for Al3+ sensing based on the A-CDs/MR-Al3+ FRET system.
Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of A-CDs/MR (100 μM) upon addition of Al3+ (50 μM) in ethanol.

The absorption spectra of MR-Al3+ conjugate (a) and fluorescence spectra of A-CDs (b) (A). Fluorescence spectra of the A-CDs and A-CDs/MR system in the presence of different amounts of Al3+ (from 0 to 70 μM), inset: The linear region (from 8 to 20 μM) of Stern-Volmer plot (B).

Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of A-CDs/MR (A). Fluorescence response of the A-CDs/MR sensing system in the presence of Al3+ with the addition of different metal ions respectively (B).

Bright-field transmission image (A) and fluorescence image (B) of HUVEC cells incubated with CDs for 6 h at 37 °C. Fluorescence image of HUVEC cells incubated with CDs and 10 μM MR (C). Fluorescence image of HUVEC cells incubated with CDs, 10 μM MR and 5 μM (D), 10 μM (E) and 20 μM Al3+ (F). Scale bar: 20 μm.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of fluorescent H-CDs and A-CDs.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of Al3+.

row.entry: Potentiometric sensor based on morin in poly (vinyl chloride) matrix
Ionic liquid–water mixtures based on morin
Fluorescent method based on carbon dots

head.ce:title: Amphiphilic carbon dots for sensitive detection, intracellular imaging of Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology/Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical College, Guizhou 563000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacology/Key Laboratory for Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple and effective method was designed to synthesize hydrophobic carbon dots. Subsequently, amphiphilic fluorescent carbon dots (A-CDs) were synthesized by further surface modification. The result A-CDs show excellent optical properties with a quantum yield of 16.9%. It was interestingly found that morin (MR) and its fluorescent metal-ion complex (MR-Al3+) can successfully coordinate on the surface of A-CDs, the emission of A-CDs completely overlapped the absorption peak of MR-Al3+. Thus, the prepared A-CDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for Al3+ based on a fluorescence resonance energy transfer process. The sensing platform can realize real-time detection of Al3+ within 0.5 min. The fluorescence signals of the system were linearly correlated with the concentration of Al3+ over a range of 8–20 μM, with a detection limit of 0.113 μM. The method was also successfully applied to image the distribution of Al3+ in Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Researches on the detection of various metal ions or anions are essential for various biologically and environmentally important species [1,2]. Aluminium as the abundant metal in the earth's crust is commonly used in our daily life. Al3+ has been widely investigated in the etiology of neurological disorders, such as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and dialysis encephalopathy [3–5]. Thus, Al3+ detection is very important to minimize the effect on human health. There have been reported some conventional methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, ICP mass spectrometry, electrochemical methods and fluorescent analysis [6–10]. However, these methods show limitations, such as need tedious pre-treatment, time-consuming processes, professional operation and high costs. It is of high significance to design simple, inexpensive and rapid methods for sensing Al3+.

Recently, the development of probes based on optical signals is attracting much more attention because of their roles in the chemical, biological and environmental researches [11–14]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new “zero-dimensional” fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted much attention because of its high photostability, tunable emission, chemical inertness, easy functionalization and low toxicity [15–21]. Due to their high fluorescence emission and wide excitation spectra, CDs are excellent donors for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system [22–25]. Recently, some groups have reported CDs-based fluorescent probes for detecting metal ions through FRET process. Kim's group designed a FRET-based Al3+ probe by immobilizing rhodamine B on the surface of CDs [26]. Upon the addition of Al3+, the fluorescent intensity of rhodamine B moieties increased because of the energy transfer from CDs. Yang et al. established a FRET system for the analysis of Zn2+. CDs were served as the donor and the quercetin-Zn(II) is served as the acceptor [27].

Inspired by the above reports, we designed a FRET system for detecting Al3+ by using amphiphilic fluorescent carbon dots (A-CDs) and morin (MR). The emission of the prepared A-CDs completely overlapped the absorption of the complex MR-Al3+. A-CDs were synthesized from citric acid anhydrous and methionine through a simple process (Scheme 1 ). It was found that the blue fluorescence of A-CDs can be quenched by MR. Upon the addition of Al3+, the mixture showed bright green color at once. The fluorescent intensity of the system increased linearly towards Al3+ in the range of 8–20 μM. Being associated with low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the method was also applied for intracellular imaging Al3+ in Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC).

Citric acid anhydrous, methionine, cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and all the other reagents were purchased from local companies (http://www.tjhxsj.cn/). Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) and morin (MR) were supplied by Aladdin. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. All metal ion stock solutions were prepared from nitrate salts or chlorate salts. The deionized water obtained from a Millipore NanoPure purification system (resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ cm−1).

The fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR37). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on an electronic microscope (HITACHI, H-7650). Particle sizes were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The absorption spectra of the samples were measured on a UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). A PHS-3W pH meter (Shanghai Lida Instrument Factory) was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an Olympus IX71 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20 × objective lens.

H-CDs were synthesized from citric acid and methionine through a thermal oxidation process in air. An acidified (with hydrochloric acid) aqueous solution of methionine (4.47 g) was slowly added into citric acid (1.92 g) solution under constant stirring. The result solution was evaporated at 100–120 °C to yield a white powder. The dried white powder was then put in a silica crucible and subjected to thermal oxidation in air for 30 min at 300 °C. After cooling down, the result product was dissolved in dry acetone and filtered to remove the undissolved components. Then H-CDs were prepared by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying. A-CDs were prepared by coating H-CDs with long-chain cationic surfactant CTAB. CTAB (0.2 g) was dissolved in 40 mL chloroform, to which previously obtained H-CDs (0.1 g) were added. The mixture was vigorously stirred at ambient temperature to enable the ion exchange between the H-CDs and the CTAB molecules. The result mixture was washed with distilled water more than 5 times to completely remove the excess CTAB. After removal solvent by rotary evaporation, the obtained solid was dissolved in water and then loaded into dialysis bag (Mw = 3000) for dialysis against distilled water for 4 days. Eventually, pure A-CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying.

A-CDs were dissolved in deionized water to afford 1.0 mg mL−1 aqueous solution. 0.1 mL A-CD solution and 0.1 mL MR (1 mM) were added to 10 mL volumetric flasks. Then Al3+ solution (2 mM) ranging from 0 to 0.4 mL were added to the above mixture solution and constant volume with ethanol. After thoroughly mixed, fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature with excitation and mission slit width of 10 and 5 nm and excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

HUVEC (bought from Genomeditech (shanghai) Co., Ltd in Shanghai, China) were cultured in medium supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. For imaging, HUVEC cells were seeded on a coverslip in 6-well plate 12 h before use. Then, 10 μg mL−1 CD solution, 10 μM MR and10 μg mL−1 CD solution, 10 μM MR, Al3+ (5 μM, 10 μM and 20 μM) and 10 μg mL−1 CD solution were added to each well, respectively. HUVEC cells and the mixed solution were co-cultured for 6 h to study the application of CDs/MR system to the detection of Al3+ in the HUVEC cells. After being washed with fresh 1 × PBS (pH = 7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on the inverted fluorescence microscope.

A-CDs were synthesized by surface modifying H-CDs with long-chain cationic surfactant (Scheme 1). As shown in Fig. S1, A-CDs successfully transferred into water phase from toluene phase and show blue-emitting. The upper toluene phase lost much blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. The morphologies of A-CDs were investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 A shows that the prepared A-CDs are monodisperse with near spherical morphologies. The elemental analysis and surface composition of A-CDs were studied by XPS (Fig. 1B). A-CDs show five apparent peaks corresponding to Br3d (68 eV), S2p (160 eV), C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV) and O1s (532 eV) respectively. The C1s spectrum centered at 285 eV can be split into three peaks which attributing to the binding energy of CC, CN and CO groups (Fig. S2A). The N1s spectrum shows two peaks around 400 eV, which are assigned to the CNC and NH bands, respectively (Fig. S2B). The spectrum of O1s was split into three peaks: CO, COH and COC groups (Fig. S2C) [28,29]. The high resolution Br3d spectrum of A-CDs in Fig. 1C reveals the Br segments of CTAB.

Surface functional groups of A-CDs were studied by FTIR (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 3423 cm−1 and 2919 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH. Two peaks at 3126 cm−1 and 1655 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of NH and CO, respectively. These results indicate the presence of COOH and NH2 on the surface of A-CDs. The results from the zeta potential measurements show that the A-CDs are negatively charged with ζ of −4.92 mV, implying the presence of surface carboxyl groups (Table S1). In addition, the peak around 1123 cm−1 is ascribed to CN and CS bands. The peak at 1400 cm−1 is due to the formation of NHCO groups during the synthesis of CDs. On the other hand, an absorption band characteristic of long-chain alkyl structure (719 cm−1) can be clearly identified in the spectra for both CTAB and A-CDs (Fig. 1D).

Fig. 2 shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of A-CDs. A-CDs exhibit strong absorbance around 250 nm, which is ascribed to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [30]. The other peak 350 nm is attributed to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [31,32]. The detailed fluorescence analyses at different excitation wavelengths reveal the excellent excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior (Fig. 2B). The λex-dependent fluorescence spectra are derived from the π∗ → n transitions of the surface-attached functional groups (CO/CNH2). The strong fluorescence emission of A-CDs is attributed to the presence of nitrogen. The nitrogen bonding to carbon disorders the carbon hexagonal rings and creates emission energy traps for the particles through the radiative recombination induced by electron–hole pairs [33–36]. The quantum yield of A-CDs is calculated to be 16.9% in water.

To investigate the stability, fluorescence intensity of the CD solution under extreme pH, high ionic strengths and stabilization time were measured. As shown in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity of A-CDs is relatively stable over a range of pH from 4 to 11. Besides, the fluorescence intensity does not change much under different concentrations of KCl. It is beneficial for applying A-CDs in practical applications in the presence of various salt concentrations (Fig. S3B). Besides, A-CDs still could exhibit bright fluorescence even after 12 days exposing to the air indicating their high storage stability (Fig. S3C).

The outstanding optical properties of the A-CDs encourage us to further study their potential applications. It was significantly found that the A-CDs can be used to form a FRET system. MR is a well-known reagent for determination of Al3+ in ethanol (Fig. S4). The association of Al3+ with MR gives rise to two complexes with 1:1 and 2:1 (MR: Al3+) stoichiometries [37] (Fig. S5). While the detection limit (LOD) of Al3+ was calculated to be 6.83 μM only. Then A-CDs were applied to detect Al3+ based on a FRET process (Fig. 3 ). The highest fluorescence intensity ratio of A-CDs: MR-Al3+ (μg mL−1: μM) was 1:1 (Fig. S6). Under the excitation of 350 nm, MR-Al3+ accepted the energy transferred from A-CDs. Then the system released the energy through the fluorescence channel and emitted the fluorescence at 502 nm.

In order to demonstrate the feasibility of the FRET based system for detecting Al3+, the fluorescence and absorption spectra of A-CDs and MR-Al3+ were investigated. The fluorescence emission of A-CDs completely overlapped the absorption spectrum of MR-Al3+ (Fig. 5A). Another important factor is the distance between the donor and the acceptor which is related to the efficiency of FRET. The length is limited to 100 Å based on the nature of the dipole−dipole mechanism [38]. Förster radius R0, the distance between donor and acceptor at a transfer efficiency of 50%, was calculated to be 96.6 Å. It was expressed by the following equation in angstroms Å:
R 0
= 0.2108 ×
[
K 2
×
φ D
×
n R
− 4
× J
( λ )
]
1 / 6
[39]. Here, K2 (2/3) is the factor expressing the relative orientation of the donor to the acceptor molecule, nR is the refractive index of the medium, ΦD (0.169) is the quantum yield of the donor in the absence of the acceptor, and J(λ) (calculated to be 1.01 × 1018 M−1 cm−1 nm4) is the degree of spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption.

In order to further understand the coordination behaviors between A-CD, MR and Al3+, titration experiment of MR to A-CD was investigated. Fig. S7 shows a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of A-CDs with the increasing MR concentration. The fluorescence of A-CDs was quenched by MR through electrostatic interaction. The surface of A-CDs contains abundant basic groups-amino groups. And the phenolic hydroxyl groups of MR belong to soft acids [40]. Thus MR could link to the surface of A-CDs and quenched the fluorescence of A-CDs. On the other hand, the zeta-potential measurements were also tested (Table S1). The zeta-potential of A-CDs was −3.99 mV, which subsequently changed to −4.37 mV after the addition of MR. The negative charge density changed to a positive surface potential with the addition of Al3+. The results suggest that both of MR and MR-Al3+ effectively coordinate to the surface of A-CDs.

Furthermore, the time dependence of the A-CDs/MR towards Al3+ was performed (Fig. 4
). No obvious fluorescence of A-CDs/MR was observed at 502 nm. When Al3+ was added into the solution of A-CDs/MR, the fluorescence increased immediately within 0.5 min. The plot leveled off at the maximum value after 15 min. These results indicated that Al3+ completely interacted with the system with 15 min.

Then we investigated the fluorescence changes of the FRET system upon different concentration of Al3+ (Fig. 5B). It can be clearly seen that A-CDs exhibit a strong emission at 420 nm. While the fluorescence intensity almost completely quenches with the addition of MR. With the increasing Al3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 502 nm of the system shows great increase. It's worth noting that Al3+ cannot quench the fluorescence of A-CDs, indicating that the changes of fluorescence are caused by the FRET process (Fig. S8). The relative fluorescence intensity exhibits a good linear response to Al3+ concentration in the range of 8–20 μM (Fig. 5B). The calibration curve can be expressed as Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F-1 = −0.8525–0.0061c (R2 = 0.9944). Here, F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of CDs/MR in the absence and presence of Al3+. The LOD of Al3+ was calculated to be 0.113 μM based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of eight blank measurements; s is absolute value of the slope of calibration curve).

To verify the feasibility of the A-CDs/MR for Al3+ recognition, potential interferences from various metal cations were investigated. In the selectivity tests, only Al3+ caused obvious fluorescence change (Fig. 6 A). While Cd2+ and Zn2+ have a little interference on the above experiment. Then we investigated the fluorescence response of the system towards Al3+ in the presence of Cd2+ and Zn2+. As shown in Fig. S9, this probe still can effectively detect Al3+ in the range of 8–20 μM. The corresponding Stern-Volmer equation has a small change which can be ignored comparing the above equation. The effects of other coexisting metal ions on Al3+ detection by A-CDs/MR were also performed (Fig. 6B). The results clearly demonstrate that the coexistence of common metal ions do not affect the complexation of Al3+ with MR. A comparison of our proposed method with some other reports about detecting Al3+ was studied. Table 1 reveals that the present method has good linear response and high sensitivity which is comparable and even better that the reported papers. All these results suggest that this sensing platform can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for Al3+.

MTT assay on HUVEC cells with A-CDs concentration ranging from 0 to 200 μg mL−1 was explored to study the cytotoxicity. A-CDs almost did not obviously reduce the cell viability (Fig. S10). In order to study the application of this system for imaging in biological system, the response of the probe to Al3+ in cultured cells was performed. The bright-field transmission of HUVEC cells reveals a clear cell image (Fig. 7 A). Then the transfected HUVEC cells became quite bright blue owing to the strong fluorescence from CDs, indicating a large amount of CDs have been internalized into the cells (Fig. 7B). The cells incubated with CDs/MR emitted weak blue fluorescence, demonstrating the penetration of CDs and MR into the cells (Fig. 7C). While the color of the cells changed to green by further cultivating the cells with Al3+, suggesting the FRET process can also take place in cells. Besides, the color change from light green to deep green revealing the feasibility of the system applied in intracellular imaging of different Al3+ concentrations (Fig. 7D, E and F). All these results reveal that the sensing platform can be applied in intracellular imaging Al3+.

In summary, A-CDs with excellent fluorescence properties were synthesized for detecting Al3+. A-CDs exhibit excellent solubility and strong fluorescence both in water and in organic solvents. The prepared A-CDs can be served as effective probe for Al3+ through FRET process. It shows high extent of spectral overlap and proper distance between the donor A-CDs and the acceptor MR-Al3+. The sensing platform shows rapid fluorescence response and high sensitivity towards Al3+. Significantly, this proposed method was also successfully applied to imagine the distribution of Al3+ in HUVEC cells. Combining these characteristics, this method has promising applications in the field of bio-imaging and bio-sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21374078, 51303132) and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100, 15JCTPJC59200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.11.049.
